title: What You Should Never Put in Compost
excerpt: Not everything organic belongs in a compost pile. Some materials attract pests, spread disease, create odor, or contaminate finished compost, so knowing what to avoid is just as important as knowing what to add.
author: Permatable Team
content:
Compost Safety

Composting is a great way to turn everyday waste into something useful for your garden, but not everything belongs in a compost pile. Some materials break down poorly, attract pests, spread disease, or create unpleasant odors that make compost harder to manage.

Knowing what not to compost is just as important as knowing what to add. When you avoid the wrong materials, your compost stays healthier, cleaner, and easier to maintain.

Why Some Materials Should Stay Out of Compost

A good compost pile depends on the right balance of ingredients. Most home compost systems work best with plant-based kitchen scraps, dry browns, and safe garden waste.

Certain materials cause problems because they can:

  • Attract rats, flies, or other pests
  • Make the pile smell bad
  • Introduce plant diseases
  • Survive the composting process
  • Slow decomposition
  • Contaminate finished compost

These risks are especially important in home composting, where piles often do not get hot enough to safely break down every type of waste.

1. Meat and Fish

Meat and fish scraps should not go into a basic home compost pile.

These materials:

  • Smell bad as they decompose
  • Attract pests and scavengers
  • Break down in an unpleasant way
  • Can make compost difficult to manage

Even small amounts can create problems, especially in open piles or backyard bins.

2. Dairy Products

Milk, cheese, yogurt, butter, and other dairy products are also poor choices for compost.

Like meat, dairy can:

  • Create strong odors
  • Attract pests
  • Become messy in the pile
  • Disrupt the balance of the compost

Most home gardeners are better off keeping dairy out of traditional compost systems.

3. Oily or Greasy Foods

Grease, cooking oil, oily leftovers, and fried foods should be avoided.

These materials can:

  • Coat compost ingredients
  • Reduce airflow
  • Create bad smells
  • Attract insects and animals

Compost works best with natural, mostly dry or moist organic materials, not heavy greasy waste.

4. Pet Waste

Dog and cat waste should not be added to regular home compost.

Pet waste can contain harmful pathogens and parasites that may survive in low-temperature compost piles. This makes it unsafe, especially if the compost will be used around edible plants.

Manure from herbivores like rabbits or chickens may be composted in some cases, but household pet waste should be kept out of standard compost.

5. Diseased Plants

Plants that are infected with fungal problems, blight, mildew, or other diseases should usually not go into a home compost pile.

The concern is that the compost may not heat up enough to kill the disease organisms. If that happens, the finished compost can spread the problem back into the garden.

It is safer to avoid composting:

  • Diseased leaves
  • Infected stems
  • Blighted tomato plants
  • Mildewed plant material

6. Weeds With Seeds

Weeds can be composted in some situations, but weeds that have gone to seed are risky.

If the compost pile does not get hot enough, the seeds may survive and spread when the compost is used in the garden.

That can create more weeding work later. For beginners, it is often best to leave out:

  • Seed heads
  • Mature weeds
  • Aggressive spreading weeds
  • Roots of invasive plants

7. Treated or Painted Wood

Wood scraps may seem compostable, but treated, painted, or chemically finished wood should never be added.

These materials can contain substances that do not belong in compost and may contaminate the finished product.

Only small amounts of untreated sawdust or plain wood shavings are safe for composting.

8. Glossy or Coated Paper

Some paper products are compostable, but glossy, heavily colored, or plastic-coated paper is not a good choice.

Avoid:

  • Laminated paper
  • Glossy magazines
  • Coated packaging
  • Paper with heavy inks or synthetic layers

Better paper choices for compost include plain newspaper, cardboard, and uncoated paper towels.

9. Large Amounts of Citrus or Onion Scraps

Citrus peels and onion scraps are not always forbidden, but large amounts can be a problem in smaller compost piles.

They break down slowly and may affect the balance of the pile if added in excess. In most home systems, small amounts are fine, but it is better not to overload the compost with them.

Moderation matters here more than total avoidance.

10. Charcoal Ash and Coal Ash

Ash can be confusing because some types are compostable and others are not.

Wood ash in small amounts may be used carefully in some gardens, but charcoal ash and coal ash should stay out of compost. These can contain substances that are not helpful or safe for garden soil.

If you are unsure what kind of ash you have, it is usually better not to add it.

11. Synthetic Materials

This may sound obvious, but it is still worth mentioning. Compost should not contain plastic, metal, glass, synthetic fabric, rubber, or anything non-biodegradable.

Even compostable packaging should only be added if you know it is suitable for home composting. Some products break down only in industrial composting facilities.

12. Large Amounts of Cooked Food

Plain cooked rice, pasta, or bread can sometimes be composted in small amounts, but large amounts of cooked food are risky.

They may:

  • Attract pests
  • Clump together
  • Create odor
  • Unbalance the pile

If you do add a small amount, bury it well and cover it with plenty of browns.

What Is Safe to Compost Instead?

If you are unsure whether something belongs in compost, it is usually safest to stick with simple materials like:

  • Fruit and vegetable scraps
  • Coffee grounds
  • Tea leaves
  • Crushed eggshells
  • Dry leaves
  • Shredded cardboard
  • Newspaper
  • Grass clippings
  • Plant trimmings

These materials are easy to manage and work well in most home compost systems.

A Simple Rule to Remember

A helpful composting rule is this: if a material is likely to smell, attract pests, spread disease, or contain chemicals, it probably does not belong in a backyard compost pile.

This rule can help you make quick decisions even when you are unsure about a specific item.

Final Thoughts

Knowing what you should never put in compost helps prevent many of the problems beginners face. Meat, dairy, oily foods, pet waste, diseased plants, treated wood, and other unsuitable materials can turn a healthy compost pile into a difficult one.

When you keep compost simple and stick to safe ingredients, the process becomes easier, cleaner, and much more effective. Good compost starts with good choices.
